draft.introduction: Jeremiah 31:31–34 announces the “new covenant”—the only place in the Hebrew Bible where this phrase appears. It became the foundational text for Christian claims of a new dispensation.

translation.text: “Behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel, not like the covenant that I made with their fathers. For this is the covenant that I will make: I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts. And I shall be their God, and they shall be my people.”
translation.original_text: ἰδοὺ ἡμέραι ἔρχονται φησὶν κύριος καὶ διαθήσομαι διαθήκην καινήν

translation.text: Thus says the Lord, who gives the sun for light by day and the fixed order of the moon and the stars for light by night: “If this fixed order departs from before me, then shall the offspring of Israel cease from being a nation before me forever.”
translation.original_text: οὕτως εἶπεν κύριος ὁ δοὺς τὸν ἥλιον

translator_notes.note: The Greek διαθήκη καινή (“new covenant”) is quoted verbatim in Hebrews 8:8-12, the longest OT quotation in the NT. The author of Hebrews argues that by calling it “new,” God made the first covenant obsolete—a reading that Jewish tradition contests.
